description: The US dollar was decoupled from gold ending the Bretton Woods system. This gave the Fed freedom to control money supply independently. It marked the beginning of the modern era of fiat currency and monetary policy.

OPEC cut oil supplies to the US triggering fuel shortages and price spikes. Inflation surged and the economy entered recession simultaneously — a new phenomenon called stagflation. The Fed struggled to respond as traditional tools were ineffective against supply-driven inflation.

Fed Chairman Paul Volcker raised interest rates to nearly 20% to break the back of inflation. The cure worked but caused two recessions and unemployment above 10%. It established the Fed's credibility as an inflation fighter and set the template for modern monetary policy.

On October 19th the stock market fell 22% in a single day — the largest one-day percentage drop in history. The crash was amplified by computerized trading and portfolio insurance strategies. The Fed responded by flooding the system with liquidity preventing a broader economic collapse.

The Fed surprised markets with a series of rapid rate hikes causing global bond prices to collapse. Investors who had borrowed heavily to buy bonds faced catastrophic losses. It was the first major demonstration of how quickly Fed policy changes could ripple through global markets.

Currency crises swept through Thailand South Korea and Indonesia as investors fled emerging markets. Contagion spread rapidly as countries with dollar-denominated debt faced impossible repayment burdens. The IMF stepped in with bailouts that came with controversial austerity conditions.

Russia defaulted on its debt sending shockwaves through global credit markets. Hedge fund Long-Term Capital Management which held massive leveraged positions collapsed requiring a Fed-coordinated bailout. It was the first major warning that financial interconnection could amplify local crises into systemic threats.

The Nasdaq fell 78% as the technology bubble burst wiping out trillions in paper wealth. Companies with no revenue or profits that had traded at astronomical valuations went bankrupt. It took 15 years for the Nasdaq to recover to its 2000 peak.

The Fed began raising rates from 1% — the lowest level in 45 years — to combat rising inflation. By 2006 rates had reached 5.25% in one of the most sustained tightening cycles of the modern era. The low rates that preceded this cycle had fueled the housing bubble that would later collapse.

The collapse of Lehman Brothers triggered a global financial crisis as interconnected banks faced insolvency. Credit markets froze and the global economy entered the worst recession since the Great Depression. Governments worldwide launched unprecedented bailouts and stimulus programs to prevent complete collapse.

Greece revealed its debt was far larger than reported triggering a sovereign debt crisis across southern Europe. Ireland Portugal Spain and Italy faced borrowing costs that threatened their ability to service existing debt. The European Central Bank eventually pledged to do whatever it takes to preserve the euro.

Fed Chairman Bernanke hinted that the central bank might slow its bond purchases causing global markets to sell off sharply. Emerging market currencies collapsed as investors anticipated higher US rates and pulled capital. It demonstrated how dependent global markets had become on continued Fed stimulus.

The Fed raised rates for the first time in nearly a decade signaling confidence in the economic recovery. The move was cautious and rates rose slowly over several years to a peak of 2.5%. Markets had become so accustomed to low rates that even small increases caused volatility.

The US imposed tariffs on Chinese goods triggering retaliatory measures and escalating trade tensions. Markets swung violently as investors tried to assess the economic impact of a potential decoupling of the two largest economies. The S&P 500 fell nearly 20% in the fourth quarter of 2018.

The pandemic caused the fastest bear market in history with the S&P 500 falling 34% in 33 days. The Fed cut rates to zero and launched the largest emergency stimulus program in its history. Markets recovered explosively as fiscal and monetary support flooded the economy.

Inflation hit 40-year highs forcing the Fed to raise rates at the fastest pace since the Volcker era. The Nasdaq fell over 30% as rising rates hit high-growth technology stocks hardest. It was the first true test of how markets built on a decade of zero rates would respond to normalization.

Inflation hit 40-year highs forcing the Fed to raise rates at the fastest pace since the Volcker era. The Nasdaq fell over 30% as rising rates hit high-growth technology stocks hardest. It was the first true test of how markets built on a decade of zero rates would respond to normalization.

The Fed began cutting rates as inflation fell toward its 2% target after the most aggressive tightening cycle in decades. Markets rallied in anticipation of easier financial conditions. The soft landing — taming inflation without causing a recession — appeared within reach.
